intro_sentence: Jovan "04" Rodriguez is a Call of Duty esports player who previously played for Vancouver Surge to start his career. After two years of competing, 04 has had four Grand Final appearances on LAN.
bio_html:
Jovan “04” Rodriguez first made his name in the Call of Duty Challengers circuit during the Modern Warfare III season. As a young rookie talent, he gained attention for his aggressive SMG playstyle and clutch SND performances at online Cups, eventually breaking through to win the Miami Open with Lore Gold. Jovan quickly proved within half a season that he was the next hot prospect.

Modern Warfare III

04 earned his CDL debut in the second half of the Modern Warfare III season, when he was signed to bolster a struggling Seattle Surge lineup. Though his rookie year was short, he quickly became a fan favorite for his fearless entry play and raw skill. He helped his squad to a surprising Top 6 finish at Major III & Major IV, cementing his status as a legitimate CDL-caliber player. While the team finished just Top 8 Champs, the team would have another chance to impress at the inaugural Esports World Cup. Behind a strong performance with his rookie Duo, Jordan “Abuzah” François, the Surge would finish Top 4 to take home $160,000. 04’s stock would rise significantly going into his Sophomore season, as he finished 4th in Rookie of the Year voting.

Black Ops 6

By Black Ops 6, 04 had become a centerpiece of the newly rebranded Vancouver Surge’s identity, with the team building around both 04 & Abuzah. Signing Englishmen Charlie “Hicksy” Hicks and Byron “Nastie” Plumridge, the team struggled at the start. Finishing last at both Minor I & Major I brought on change, with Travis “Neptune” McCloud being acquired to take Hicksy’s starting role. Now with a fast-paced SMG duo, the squad would go on to make four Grand Finals over the final five LAN tournaments. Although the team failed to win any Championships, they continually developed into a top team within the Call of Duty League. 04’s consistent development placed him firmly in the conversation as one of the league’s next star players heading into Black Ops 7.

intro_sentence: Brendan "2ReaL" Stockdale is a Call of Duty Challengers player, previously playing within the CDL with the Florida Mutineers & Las Vegas Legion.
bio_html:
Brendan "2ReaL" Stockdale began his competitive Call of Duty career in the Challengers circuit during the 2018 season, debuting at the CWL Las Vegas Open. He first competed under the Nemesis banner, originally going by the gamertag Camila2RCabello before transitioning to the name 2ReaL.

CDL Challengers Run

Over the next four seasons in Tier 2, 2ReaL steadily improved his results before finally breaking through during the Vanguard season (2021–22). That year, he captured both his first Open title at the CDC Toronto Open and his first Elite championship in Stage 4 NA. Off the back of those standout performances, the Florida Mutineers signed him for the remainder of the season. While the team placed Top 6 at the final Major, they ultimately missed out on qualifying for CoD Champs, ending their season.

Second Pro League Stint

Entering Modern Warfare 2, 2ReaL returned to Challengers following his brief stint in the Pro League. With Decimate Gaming, he claimed the CDC Boston Open championship, reigniting his stock during rostermania. Soon after, the Las Vegas Legion signed him for Stage 3 of the Pro League season. The team placed Top 6 at Major III, but 2ReaL was benched following the event.

Rebuilding

Across the next two seasons, 2ReaL continued to compete in Challengers, determined to earn another shot in the CDL. His most notable achievements came in the Challengers Finals, where he won the championship during Modern Warfare 3 and followed it up with a 3rd-place finish in Black Ops 6.

intro_sentence: Patrick "ACHES" Price is a retired professional Call of Duty player and two-time World Champion, having claimed the titles in 2014 and 2018, along with 17 other Major tournament victories. Widely regarded as one of the scene’s most accomplished leaders, he served as the captain and in-game leader for storied teams including LeveraGe, compLexity, Evil Geniuses, and FaZe Clan.
bio_html:
Modern Warfare 2 (2009–2010)

Although ACHES began competing during Call of Duty 4: Modern Warfare, it was Modern Warfare 2 where his professional career truly took off. His first major team was LeveraGe, where he began his long-standing partnership with TeePee. Alongside Fatalize and Carnage, the squad competed at the MLG Online National Championship before reshaping the roster with Miles and Juhgee. That lineup went on to place 2nd at the MLG National Championship 2010, earning ACHES his first notable payday.

Black Ops (2010–2011)

With Black Ops, ACHES brought back LeveraGe under Quantic sponsorship, forming Quantic LeveraGe with TeePee, Bobby, and FEARS. They quickly established themselves as a powerhouse, winning the $25K Online MLG tournament and following it up with a championship at MLG Dallas after replacing FEARS with a young Scump. A midseason swap of Bobby for Proofy led to another title at MLG Anaheim. However, as the year went on, consistency waned, and the roster closed the season with disappointing 5th–6th place finishes.

Modern Warfare 3 (2011–2012)

Due to a lack of developer and MLG support, competitive Modern Warfare 3 was limited. ACHES and TeePee attended only one international event, EGL 8 in the UK, teaming with John and Nadeshot. They placed 8th, marking ACHES’s worst LAN placing at the time, and he sat out most of the season.

Black Ops 2 (2012–2013)

With competitive support at an all-time high, ACHES joined compLexity Gaming alongside TeePee, FEARS, and Crimsix. The team made an immediate impact, winning Frag Cup IV and finishing 2nd at UMG Chicago. After a high-profile altercation with OpTic’s Nadeshot, FEARS was dropped in favor of TuQuick, but the roster struggled at the Call of Duty Championship. Clayster replaced TuQuick, and the new lineup dominated the latter half of the season, winning the MLG Spring Championship, Gfinity 1, UMG Atlanta, Gfinity 2, the MLG Fall Invitational, and ESWC. By the end of Black Ops 2, compLexity were recognized as one of the era’s best teams.

Ghosts (2013–2014)

Building on their momentum, compLexity won the MLG Fall Championship and replaced Clayster with Karma. The team went on a historic run, winning UMG Philadelphia and most notably the Call of Duty Championship 2014, where they claimed $400,000. Shortly after, compLexity was acquired by Evil Geniuses, where ACHES continued his dominance with victories at UGC Niagara and MLG Anaheim. Despite setbacks, including suspensions and roster turbulence, ACHES led EG to close the season with a win at ESWC 2014, solidifying his reputation as one of CoD’s greatest in-game leaders.

Advanced Warfare (2014–2015)

Amid contract issues with EG, ACHES joined FaZe Clan alongside Censor, Apathy, and SlasheR. Despite doubts, FaZe dramatically won MLG Columbus 2014, ACHES famously playing through a severe hand injury to upset OpTic Gaming in a nine-map Grand Final. He later competed under FaZe Red, placing 3rd at the 2015 Call of Duty Championship. However, inconsistent results with multiple rosters across FaZe, Prophecy, TCM, and Rise Nation left Advanced Warfare as a turbulent season for him.

Black Ops 3 (2015–2016)

ACHES joined Team EnVyUs with SlasheR, TeePee, and JKap, but mixed results led to roster changes. Mid-season, he transitioned to 100 Thieves, though their debut at MLG Anaheim was disastrous (17th–20th). Later, ACHES signed with Cloud9, joining Assault, Ricky, and Lacefield. The team made a surprising run at the 2016 Call of Duty Championship, eliminating OpTic Gaming before finishing 5th–6th overall.

Infinite Warfare (2016–2017)

Remaining with Cloud9, ACHES opened the year strong with a 2nd-place finish at CWL Las Vegas 2017, but inconsistency plagued the roster. After a string of poor finishes, Ricky and Lacefield were replaced with Priest and Xotic, which revitalized the team. Under ACHES’s leadership, Cloud9 qualified for Stage 2 of the Global Pro League and shocked the community with a 4th-place finish at CWL Anaheim 2017.

World War II (2017–2018)

ACHES began the WWII season with Team EnVyUs, but the roster failed to find consistency in the early events, leading to his eventual departure. Midway through the year, he was recruited to Evil Geniuses, where he reunited with longtime teammate Apathy, alongside Assault and SiLLY. This lineup steadily improved throughout the year, culminating in a miracle run at the 2018 Call of Duty Championship. ACHES, serving as the team’s leader, guided EG to the title, his second World Championship and one of the biggest underdog stories in CoD history.

Black Ops 4 (2018–2019)

Looking to carry momentum into Black Ops 4, Evil Geniuses struggled to replicate their Champs magic. Despite fielding several rosters over the year, including players like Temp, Nameless, and Faccento, the team consistently underperformed. After a disappointing season, ACHES parted ways with EG, closing a turbulent chapter in his career.

Modern Warfare (2019–2020)

The launch of the Call of Duty League (CDL) introduced franchising, and ACHES signed with the Los Angeles Guerrillas (LAG) as their inaugural captain. While his leadership experience was invaluable, the Guerrillas struggled to start the season, finishing at Week 2 in London. Roster instability and a steep increase in overall league competition limited ACHES’s impact, and he was benched early in the season. By the end of Modern Warfare, his time as a CDL starter had effectively come to a close.

Retirement & Legacy

Following the Modern Warfare season, Patrick “ACHES” Price officially stepped away from competing, marking the end of one of the most decorated and controversial careers in Call of Duty history. Over the course of a decade, he earned two World Championships (2014, 2018) and nearly 20 major titles, cementing his reputation as one of the winningest in-game leaders the esport has ever seen. Known equally for his strategic mind and fiery personality, ACHES often found himself at the center of roster drama and league controversies, yet his ability to rally teams and win on the biggest stages was undeniable.

After retiring, ACHES transitioned into analysis, development work with Ubisoft, and content creation with the Flank, where he brought his deep game knowledge and candid perspective to broadcasts and podcasts. While he no longer competes, his influence continues to shape the Call of Duty League as one of its most iconic figures. For many, ACHES’s career is remembered as the blueprint of a true competitor: passionate, polarizing, but ultimately a proven champion who helped define competitive Call of Duty’s first decade.

intro_sentence:
Ronald "Abe" Abraham is a Call of Duty League player. To start 2026 he was a starting player for the Vancouver Surge.

He would then win Challengers Major 3 Open on Bush Gaming.

Abe is now a starting player in the CDL for Toronto KOI.

Jordan "Abuzah" François is a Call of Duty esports player who previously played for Vancouver Surge to start his career. Winning Rookie of the Year in Modern Warfare III (2025), Abuzah has four LAN Grand Finals appearances in two years.

hometown: Al Qassim, Al Bukayriyah, Kingdom of Saudi Arabia

intro_sentence: Kahari "Kuhlect" Miller is a Halo player who has notably competed on Cloud9, Sentinels, and Spacestation. Going into Black Ops 7, Kuhlect will be making the switch to compete in Call of Duty.

Kaci "Lqgned" Sabri is an ex-Halo player who has won Championships with OpTic Gaming & Spacestation Gaming. Lqgend won the Halo Infinite 2024 MVP, performing excellently in his first season in North America. Going into Black Ops 7, he will be making the switch to compete in Call of Duty & playing for the Vancouver Surge.

Ethan "Precision" Friese is a Halo player who has notably competed on Envy, Complexity, and Sentinels. Precision previously competed in Gears of War, winning MVP & 4 Championships. Going into Black Ops 7, Precision will be making the switch to compete in Call of Duty.

Jonathan "Renegade" Willette is a Halo player who has won Championships with OpTic Gaming, FaZe Clan, Cloud9, & more. Renegade won the Halo Infinite MVP, putting up consistent superstar performances over the last four seasons. Going into Black Ops 7, he will be making the switch to compete in Call of Duty.

Chris "Simp" Lehr is a Call of Duty esports player, currently a player for Atlanta FaZe. He is a 2019 World Champion, 2019 World Championship MVP and 2021 World Champion. He previously competed under the name Simplicity.
Jackson "Yakzn" Johnson is a Halo player who has notably competed on Envy & Luminosity. Going into Black Ops 7, Yakzn will be making the switch to compete in Call of Duty.
